title: Selecting replacement ewes from your own flock
subtitle: Which ewe lambs to retain and what to look for.
excerpt: Breeding your own replacements gives you control over flock genetics, reduces biosecurity risk from bought-in stock, and makes the flock incrementally more suited to your land. The selection criteria are straightforward: mothering ability, growth rate, structural soundness, and a clean health history in the ewe's dam.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb ewe selection guidance, ahdb.org.uk. SAC Consulting flock improvement notes. Seymour, J., The New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency (1976, updated), for smallholder breeding philosophy.

glossaryTerms.definition: A ewe lamb or hogget retained from within the flock to replace older ewes culled for age, performance, or health problems.
A numerical assessment of a sheep's fat cover, scored 0 to 5 by handling the backbone and ribs. Used to assess nutritional status and reproductive readiness.
A ewe's freedom from conformation defects that would impair her working life: straight legs, sound feet, correct jaw alignment, functional teats, and a well-attached udder.

content.text: A
replacement ewe
retained from within the flock reduces biosecurity risk and builds a closed flock that works on your land, in your conditions. Buying in replacements carries disease risk.
What to select for

The most important single criterion is the performance of the ewe lamb's dam. A ewe who consistently raises twins to good weaning weights, who milks well without mastitis problems, and who has sound feet is worth breeding from. Her daughters carry a strong genetic foundation before you've looked at anything else.
Growth rate. A ewe lamb that is substantially smaller than her contemporaries at weaning rarely catches up. Select from the upper half of the weight range for the group.
Dam history. Dam's feet, mastitis record, twin-rearing success. If you keep records, these are on your holding register.
Teats. Check both teats are present and functional. Any inverted or supernumerary teat is a reason to cull from the replacement group; it won't improve.
Feet. The foot structure of the dam and the lamb herself. Deep cleft, overgrown walls, and soft white horn are all signs to select against.

Jaw alignment. Run your finger along the incisor teeth against the dental pad. Undershot (teeth overhanging the pad, 'parrot mouth') or overshot jaws will worsen with age and compromise grazing efficiency.
Condition scoring the candidates
Condition score
each candidate at the time of selection, typically September in a lowland flock. A
body condition score
of 3 to 3.5 in a growing ewe lamb is reasonable. Score below 2 at six months of age suggests she has been outcompeted at feed or has a health issue that bears investigation.
Structural soundness check
Before confirming a lamb as a replacement, carry out a
structural soundness
check. Walk her toward you and away from you. Watch the front feet for any outward toe-turning or knuckling. Look at the hind quarters for narrow pelvis (relevant in later lambing). The
hoof trimming
tutorial covers what good foot structure looks like.

On a small flock of under twenty ewes, you might retain two or three lambs per year. Select more than you need initially, keep perhaps five candidates through the first winter and make the final cut in spring when you have seen them in harder conditions.
